data.title: Target's Dividend, A Dream Or A Trap? Earnings Preview
data.description:
Introduction

Target's shareholders expect volatility. On Tuesday, March 4, Target will hold its earnings call and option trading implies a share price swing of 9%. Day traders will carefully watch the stock to profit from these sharp movements, in case they materialize. But, aside from this, as long-term investors, how should we approach the earnings report? In this earnings preview, let's put together some data that are already available to understand what the company may release and where it is headed.

If you closely follow Target (NYSE:TGT), you have probably not seen me cover it often. In fact, I have only written on this stock twice. The first time was back in 2022 when retailers were hit by shoplifting as inflation was rising. I compared Target to a retailer I owned at that time and that I still own (I actually plan to hold on to my shares until my kids will inherit them): Costco (COST). I explained why shoplifting was a threat for Target, while Costco's business model somewhat insulated the wholesaler from it. My rating was a buy for Costco and a hold for Target. I came back to Target in November, as I wanted to check how the company was managing its inventory problems and combating shrink. I found a company focused on efficiency, but still trading at a rather lofty valuation for the value it could deliver. The stock was then trading above $150, and it has lost 19% since then.

Now, I think Seeking Alpha analyst Ken Taylor has explained quite well why Target underperforms Costco and the industry: "it is not a top dog" and "it is also not an also-ran". He then points out that both Target's e-commerce and ad businesses are playing catch-up mode, competing against companies like Amazon and Walmart. However, let's see if the numbers confirm this impression or not.

Target's Q4 Earnings Preview

A bit of macroeconomic data. According to the U.S. Department of Commerce, consumer spending in January was -0.2%. However, this doesn't affect Target's quarterly report because it focuses on October, November, and December. Well, in these three months, consumer spending was rather strong and reported three increases of 0.4%, 0.5%, and 0.8% respectively. This means Target saw a favorable environment, while it may warn investors about a softer than expected Q1.

As a recent report from McKinsey & Company shows, consumer spending in the U.S. is generally optimistic, but this year consumers are not carrying their holiday spending habits into the new year as they did in 2024. A notable trend is the continuation of trade down habits and moderation in semi-discretionary goods purchases (vehicles, beauty products, toys). This may affect Target as well, because it is usually perceived as a moderately expensive retailer and not the one where the best value is found.

At the end of Q3, Target reported revenues of $25.67B (+1.06% YoY) which came short of expectations by over $230M. It also missed EPS targets by $0.45 reporting $1.85. The consequence is widely known: the stock fell 17% in one day. The few positives were an operating income growth of 6.7%, reflecting cost savings and efficiencies, and a traffic growth of 2.4%, though the average ticket declined, showing consumers are spending more cautiously. Another improvement was a growth of nearly 11% of the digital sales channel, with same-day deliveries increasing by 20%. Target also said it wants to keep investing in its stores and its e-commerce and wants to resume growing its store count, which has been more or less flat at around 1,950 for the last three years.

In Q3, East Coast and Gulf port strikes didn't help Target's costs, but in any case, the difficulty to grow its top-line was evident before everyone's eyes.

As Target's Q4 earnings approach, analysts seem to have already digested the record-high sales that the company reported during the holiday season, with the reported earnings that could show a meaningful acceleration in discretionary merchandise. However, some concerns hinge on succession considerations and the pullback in discretionary demand that we have seen spreading among consumers as 2025 kicked in.

So, while I think it is highly predictable that Target will report comp sales growth and a traffic increase, what will determine the impact of this report on the stock will mainly depend on the FY2025 guidance.

Right now, analysts are expecting a moderate 3% decline in quarterly revenue to $30.85B and a 24% EPS contraction to $2.25. However, in the last 3 months, we have 24 favorable revisions and only four negative ones. Probably, the current consensus still doesn't fully reflect the strong holiday season, and this is why I believe Target is more likely to beat estimates this time than a quarter ago.

Now, Target is also on the radar of many investors because of its enticing dividend that currently yields 3.6%. The dividend has grown for 56 years, and it has compounded at an 11.2% annual rate over the last five years. However, the big boost came from 2020 to 2022, when the quarterly dividend of $0.66 was quickly raised to $0.68, $0.90 and $1.08. Since then, Target has increased the dividend by $0.02 per year. The next dividend hike should come in the summer, and if the $0.02 hike is confirmed, we would have a dividend increase of 1.8%, below the current inflation rate. The payout ratio is 47% and this makes the dividend relatively safe. The balance sheet, after all, carries around $20B in total debt, while the net debt is around $16.5B, Target's EBITDA is over $9B, so the debt/EBITDA ratio is just above 2x. Moreover, Target's interest expenses are moving down and in the last twelve months amount to $428M (FY23: $502M; FY22: $478M).

Valuation

Target's multiples have clearly compressed during these months as the stock sold off. Right now, its fwd PE multiple is 14.3; the fwd EV/EBITDA ratio is 8.8, and the fwd P/FCF is 8.4. The FCF yield is 7.4%, which is quite attractive. These are, generally speaking, good multiples and can, in fact, suggest Target could be an interesting opportunity in today's market. However, the discount Target trades at compared to some of the best retailers in the U.S. is somewhat deserved. Its growth prospects are still under scrutiny, up to the point analysts don't expect Target to report a quarter of growth in a few days. Even its high FCF yield comes with a reason: the company won't probably grow its FCF at a regular and steady pace. Therefore, the stock is trading down so that investors can earn some decent returns, at least from the dividend. In today's market, Target may be an opportunity. Chances are that a turnaround will materialize and investors will flock back into the stock. But right now, the variables are too many, and I would argue that the risk of a new post-earnings drop looms on the horizon. I suggest to income-oriented investors to keep an eye on the stock because if it falls another 10%-15%, its yield will truly become interesting. But for now, better to stay on the sidelines and wait for the company to give more information regarding its prospects.

data.title: Teleflex's Bold Transformation: Why I'm Skeptical On Value Creation
data.description:
I can't fault Teleflex (NYSE:TFX) management for at least trying to change course with the business. After years and years of mediocre performance, management has announced a significant acquisition and a plan to split the business, with "Teleflex Classic" going forward as a company focused on vascular care and surgical devices.

Unfortunately, I don't think these moves will add all that much value, and the Street really didn't like the news, dropping the shares over 20% on the news, and taking the decline since my last update, to over 40%.

I can make an argument for Teleflex being undervalued, but it takes more than just undervaluation to make a stock work. This next year is looking pretty lackluster in terms of growth and margin leverage, and management here has really not earned much benefit of the doubt. I very much appreciate the urge to go dumpster-diving, and I'm not sure there's all that much downside risk at this point (unless the market itself rolls over), but I'm not really into recommending stocks where I don't like the underlying trends nor really believe in management.

A Quick Review Of Earnings And Guidance

There's a lot to discuss with Teleflex now, so I'm going to give a quick flyby of fourth quarter earnings and 2025 guidance.

Revenue rose about 3% this quarter, missing by more than 2%, with only the Surgical (up 12%) and OEM (up almost 4%) businesses outperforming. Vascular revenue rose 2%, Interventional rose 19%, Anesthesia declined 2%, and Urology declined a further 9%, with ongoing weakness in UroLift only partly offset by growth in Barrigel.

Gross margin was flat from the prior year and down 70bp qoq at 60.1%. Operating income rose 8%, and beat slightly, with margin up 130bp to 27.6%.

Teleflex has been seeing deceleration in the business, and that is continuing into 2025. Management guided to just 1% to 2% revenue growth in 2025 (versus an expectation of closer to 5%), with headwinds from value-based procurement and changes to the OEM business (including the loss of a meaningful contract). On top of that, the company just isn't executing particularly well in areas like Vascular, Anesthesia, and Urology, with company performance below sector procedure trends (though that's less true for Vascular).

Guidance further indicates weaker, flattish gross margin in 2025 and a small contraction in operating margin (around 30bp). Guidance does not include the potential new 25% tariff on goods imported from Mexico, and with around 30% of manufacturing in Mexico, there could be a double-digit hit to earnings from that tariff.

Acquiring BIOTRONIK May Not Help As Much As Management Thinks

With earnings, management announced that it would be acquiring BIOTRONIK's vascular intervention business for about $793M. This business includes a range of drug-coated ballons, drug-eluting stents, bare metal and covered stents, catheters, and other access and intervention products, with a roughly 75% / 25% mix between coronary and peripheral interventions.

Teleflex is paying around 2x revenue, but that's not really the bargain that it may seem to be. The business is growing at a mid-single-digit rate and generating low double-digit operating margins. While much more competitive in European markets (BIOTRONIK is a German company), they're not a major competitive threat to companies like Abbott (ABT), Boston Scientific (BSX), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), or Medtronic (MDT).

Abbott's vascular business is about twice as large (and growing faster), while Boston Scientific's business is more than triple the size of BIOTRONIK and growing at a double-digit rate. While these aren't entirely fair comparisons, the reality is that investors in these large vascular franchises don't spend much time fretting about what BIOTRONIK is doing.

I do see some synergies for Teleflex. Adding these assets could help drive increased sales of Teleflex products in Europe and vice versa, and BIOTRONIK's Freesolve sirolimus eluting resorbable magnesium stent could offer some upside if a U.S. pivotal study is successful (I'm skeptical, and it'll be years before we know). I also do think that BIOTRONIK will help Teleflex expand its footprint in peripheral access, and that's pretty much a must-have these days.

Teleflex is claiming accretion in Year 1 and double-digit ROI for the deal, and I'm skeptical on both points. I'm more willing to go with the idea of Year 1 accretion given the price paid, but I'm skeptical about the long-term merits of the deal, even if Teleflex paid relatively little in terms of multiple (though given that EBITDA margin is likely in the mid-teens, fair value would have only been around 2.5x to 3x at best, particularly given the company's smaller market presence against some true giants).

Splitting The Business Makes Sense, But NewCo Could Have A Rough Road Ahead

Last and not least, Teleflex announced it would be undertaking a split of the business, separating into RemainCo (what I previously called "Teleflex Classic"), which will include the Vascular, Interventional, and Surgical businesses, and NewCo which will include Urology, Acute Care (Anesthesia and Respiratory), and the OEM business.

The split makes some sense, as RemainCo's points of call for sales are hospitals, while NewCo has a lot more office-based procedures. It's also worth noting that NewCo's businesses are slower-growing and lower-margin than RemainCo. While RemainCo should be able to generate mid-single-digit revenue growth (6% or higher, possibly) with a mid-60% gross margin, NewCo is likely to be a low-single-digit grower with mid-50%'s GPM. While lower R&D requirements will help operating margins, the OEM business doesn't have a particularly outstanding competitive moat.

I don't think cutting the NewCo assets loose will harm RemainCo, though there will still be ongoing questions about whether the addition of BIOTRONIK's assets and a new management focus will meaningfully improve the company's market share and revenue growth. On the flip side, NewCo could struggle to get an attractive multiple, as it will be a smallish company (around $1.3B in '24 revenue) with weak growth and margin leverage prospects that may well struggle to get a good multiple as a publicly traded stock.

The Outlook

For now, I'm modeling and valuing the business as if the BIOTRONIK deal will go through and before the split. Revenue should grow over 6% over the next three and five years, boosted by the deal, with organic growth likely more in the 3% to 3.5% range. EBITDA margin should head to 30% over the next five years, and while I once thought that 20%-plus free cash flow margins were possible over the long term, I've switched to more of a "show me" stance there, and I'm only modeling mid-to-high teens for FCF margin.

Taking all of that into account, I can argue that Teleflex shares may well be undervalued by 20% or more on discounted free cash flow and 25% or more on EV/revenue (using a 3.25x multiple). That multiple is arguably harsh for a business with comparatively strong EBITDA margins, but the weakening growth outlook drives a discount.

The Bottom Line

I do think that today's price likely undervalues these businesses as going concerns. By the same token, I understand why the Street is frustrated with this name and skeptical about this significant shift in direction, as well as a large M&A transaction that may not add the hoped-for business or financial leverage. I can appreciate the appeal of this stock now to investors who think that the reaction was overblown and captures a lot of the downside risk, but I'd prefer to own stocks that have more positives going for them than just "it's really cheap and all the bad news is probably in the stock now".

data.title: Can Sentiment Get Any Worse Than This?
data.description:
I have been trying to assess the damage from the daily barrage of tariff-related rhetoric over the past five trading days, knowing that markets lead fundamentals, while sentiment is more coincident. The most stunning aspect of what feels like a horrible month for the stock market is that the S&P 500 has pulled back just 5% from its all-time high. In fact, it is still up 1.2% this year after Friday’s month-end rally. Yet, investor sentiment has collapsed to levels consistent with ones seen previously during recessions and after bear market declines in the broad market. This is highly unusual, and I don’t have an explanation other than the extraordinarily divisiveness environment that exists today, which is clearly pushing respondents to surveys to extremes on both sides.

Even so, by these measures, it really can’t get much worse. The CNN Fear & Greed Index, which is a composite of sentiment-related variables, has plunged into Extreme Fear territory.

The index is now at the level (18) where it bottomed out last August after the S&P 500 came close to a 10% correction.

According to the American Association of Individual Investors, the percentage of bears has risen to a multi-year high of more than 60%, and there have only been six other weeks in this survey’s history when the percentage was higher. Those coincided with the recession of 1990, Iraq’s invasion of Kuwait, just after the Great Financial Crisis, and at the near bottom of the bear market in September 2022.

The percentage of bulls has plummeted to just 19%, which matches the low in March 2023 when Silicon Valley Bank went bust, and there were deep concerns about a regional banking crisis.

At this point, we don’t have a crisis. We do have a lot of fear of what is to come, due to the Trump administration’s warnings about job losses and punitive tariffs, which has severely damaged consumer sentiment. There are signs in the real economy that the rhetoric is impacting business activity, even though no tariffs have taken effect yet, which is a legitimate concern. The 10% tariff on China imports took effect on February 2, but the de minimis provision was paused, meaning that items valued at less than $800 are exempt. This is because there was mass postal chaos, leading to tremendous delays in delivery from the start. Therefore, there is very little subject to tariffs today.

Regardless, we have seen disappointing retail sales for January, while S&P Global’s PMI (Purchasing Managers Index) for the service sector fell into contraction for the first time in two years, and unemployment claims spiked in the past week. We also learned that personal spending in February declined 0.2% when it was expected to increase 0.2%, leading to a downward revision to first-quarter GDP. The Atlanta Fed now sees the economy contracting by 1.5%, which is a massive revision from the 2.3% growth expected the week before. This has far more to do with trade than spending, which is a double-edged sword.

While the decline in consumer spending did weigh on the rate of economic growth, it was the massive 25% increase in our trade deficit with the rest of the world that led to the negative number, subtracting 3.7% from the overall rate of growth. Why?

It appears that companies were importing as many raw materials and other goods as possible to front run the tariffs expected to take effect early in 2025. Therefore, if the Trump administration backs off the Draconian proposals, this could reverse overnight as we work off bloated inventories and our exports hold steady. The trade deficit could end up being a surplus to move GDP back into positive territory in March.

Yet, there is another scenario that is now my gravest concern. Beyond the implementation of the tariffs, which nearly every trading partner has said will be met with retaliatory tariffs, the harsh rhetoric is leading to boycotts of U.S. exports. This is most clear in Canada. If the tariffs proposed take effect this week, there is a significant risk that our trade deficit will worsen, due to the boycott of our goods by foreign consumers. That runs completely counter to the prime objective of the tariff policy, which is to narrow trade deficits.

It appears that investor sentiment is already prepared for the worst-case scenario. It does not appear that markets are aligned with that sentiment. My hope is that a lessening of the rhetoric alongside less aggressive trade policies will help lift sentiment this coming week. If not, then the market has more work to do on the downside to align with current sentiment readings.

data.title: RYLD: 12%+ Dividend Yields Likely To Sustain As Volatility Returns
data.description:
RYLD ETF: dividend yield now exceeds 12%

I last analyzed the Global X Russell 2000 Covered Call ETF (RYLD) back in 2023. More specifically, I published an article entitled “QYLD Or RYLD? There's A Better Idea” in September 2023. As the title already hinted, the focus of the article is to compare RYLD to other alternative funds. The comparison concluded with a hold rating for RYLD based on the following considerations:

Many income investors are familiar with Global X Russell 2000 Covered Call ETF and Global X NASDAQ 100 Covered Call ETF. Both funds currently provide ~12% dividend yields, paid monthly. This article will analyze why such yields are not as attractive as they appear on the surface. Macroscopic parameters such as high interest rates and low volatility index are likely to cause a decline in payouts. A fund with a more reasonable valuation and better price appreciation potential such as RYLG is better in my view under current conditions.

Since then, there have been many changes in the macroeconomic parameters ranging from interest rates to updated earnings reports. In the remainder, I will focus on the two changes most relevant to RYLD in my opinion: its current dividend yield and also the implied volatility (“IV”) of its underlying index (the Russell 2000 index). These factors have led me to adjust my rating on the ETF to buy from my earlier hold rating.

I will start with the simpler of these two factors: its yield. The chart below illustrates the dividend yield of RYLD since its inception in 2019. As seen, its yield fluctuated most of the time between 4% and 12% since inception. The average yield is around 6.31%. The current yield of 12.3% is almost double the historical average, indicating a low risk premium and a very attractive return/risk ratio.

Moreover, as to be argued next, I see good reasons for such a high yield to persist in the near future considering the current IV of its underlying index.

RYLD ETF: Basic facts

To prepare of the next discussion, it’s helpful to discuss the basics of RYLD and how it generates income first. As aforementioned, the RYLD fund was launched in 2019 and issued by Mirae Asset Global Investments Co. The fund tracks the CBOE Russell 2000 Buy-Write Index. It charges an expense ratio of 0.60% and manages $1.46 billion of assets under management (AUM).

To generate income, RYLD writes covered call options on the Russell 2000 index. More details of this mechanism are quoted below from the fund’s description:

RYLD fund description: RYLD tracks the Russell 2000 index while holding a succession of one-month at-the-money option of the index. The fund follows a buy-write (or covered call) strategy on the underlying index, where it buys the constituent securities of the index, or purchases other investments with substantially identical economic characteristics. At the same time, the fund writes (or sells) call options that correspond to the index.

The premium collected from the calls is the primary source of income that funds its generous dividends. With this understanding, the next section will explain why I see good odds for its current 12%+ yield to sustain (or even rise) in the near future.

RYLD ETF and Small-cap’s implied volatilities

The premium of a call options increases with the IV of the underlying assets, the Russell 2000 index in RYLD’s case. Lower IV corresponds to lower income (and thus lower yield) and vice versa. The IV underlying the Russell 2000 index is quite low in my model. As an example, the following chart (provided by MarketChameleon) shows the statistics of the IV of IWM (which I use to approximate the Russell 2000 index here) in the past 52 weeks. As seen, the current IV of IWM is 23.9, corresponding to 87th percentile of the past year. Put it another way, only 13% of the time in the past year, the index’s IV was higher than the current level. I expected such elevated IV to provide a very favorable pricing environment for RYLD’s options to support its income generation and dividend payouts.

Other risks and final thoughts

To provide a more balanced view on the ETF, I will point out a few downside risks before closing. First, if you recall from an earlier chart, RYLD charges a fee of 0.6%, which is many times more than the fees charged by many ETFs in the small-cap space. For example, IWM charges a fee of 0.19% and IJR charges 0.06%. This difference can result in a fee drag for investors who hold RYLD for the long term.

Second, the obligation for the sellers of the calls (i.e., RYLD in this case) puts a limit on the upside of the overall fund. As an example, the chart below compares the total return of RYLD to the IWM ETF in the past 5 years from 2021 to 2025. As seen, RYLD delivered a respectable total return of 26.29% in this period, again, mostly through dividends. In contrast, IWM’s total return is 52.01%, significantly outperforming RYLD.

All told, my verdict is that the positives are more than sufficient to counter the negatives under the current conditions. The top positives in my model are the unusually high dividend yield (near the peak level since its inception) and also the heightened IV of its underlying index (currently in the 87th percentile of the past 52 weeks). These positives added up to a favorable return/risk profile and promoted me to upgrading its rating to BUY.

data.title: Viridian Therapeutics: Positive Data In TED, Strong Cash Position, 1.5-Year Window
data.description:
I have been covering Viridian Therapeutics (VRDN) for a while now. VRDN has two programs, one targeting Thyroid Eye Disease or TED using an anti-IGF-1R molecule called VRDN-001, or Veligrotug (note - its subcutaneous version is called VRDN-003), and the other, a set of two FcRn inhibitors targeting various autoimmune diseases. In my last coverage 5 months back, I discussed the chronology of the data they have produced, and how that stacked up against Amgen's (AMGN) Tepezza, the only approved drug in TED, and a blockbuster revenue generator.

We saw that, initially, VRDN-001's 10mg/kg cohort produced solid interim phase 1/2 data, with "strong proptosis (bulging eyes) reduction in 83% of patients" and complete resolution of diplopia or double vision in 75% of patients. However, the 20mg/kg cohort, and especially the topline data from this same trial saw large drops in these benefits at week 6. While numerically superior to Tepezza's data, this new data hurt VRDN stock and demonstrated - tentatively - that Veligrotug's activity does not last.

Now, active TED has 25000 patients, where Tepezza is approved, and chronic Tepezza has 75000 patients, where Tepezza is not actually approved. In September last year, the company published data from the Thrive trial, which targets active TED. That data showed that Veligrotug had a week 15 proptosis responder rate (PRR) of 70% versus 6% of placebo (p < 0.0001). Likewise, the molecule met all secondary endpoints with high stat sig (p < 0.0001). There was complete resolution of diplopia in 54% of patients. Veligrotug was generally well-tolerated. Obviously, this positive trial brought Viridian back on track. While we still have to explain that anomalous data from the earlier trial, Viridian gives us more confidence now.

More good news followed soon after. I noted earlier that the Thrive trial was for active TED. There was another phase 3 trial, Thrive-2, for chronic TED -the larger market with no approved drug - and this one produced positive data in December. The study met all its primary and secondary endpoints, with "a week 14 proptosis responder rate of 56% (p < 0.0001). It also demonstrated a diplopia responder rate of 56% (placebo-adjusted rate of 31%, p = 0.0006) and 32% rate of diplopia complete resolution (placebo-adjusted rate of 18%, p = 0.0152)." The company is planning a BLA in 2H 2025.

Going into more granular details of the Thrive-2 trial, here are the key takeaways:

  • First molecule to demonstrate a diplopia response and resolution rate in a phase 3 TED study

  • Only 5 diffusions

  • Consistent data between active and chronic TED trials

  • Rapid onset of response, with statistically significant PRR at all-time points, including as early as 3 weeks after just one infusion.

  • Both MRI/CT and exophthalmometry measurements of PRR were consistent, a point that I noted about the phase 1/2 topline data.

  • Rapid onset of diplopia response as early as 6 weeks after just two infusions.

The company is also running two phase 3 trials of VRDN-003, which will produce data in 1H 2026. There are a number of derisking factors to this asset, including the fact that VRDN-003 uses the same binding domain as VRDN-001, so it is expected to be a full antagonist of IGF-1R. It also has a confirmed half life extension, so it can be dosed less frequently. The product, if approved, will come with an autoinjector, so patients can dose themselves at home.

TED market and competition

Thyroid Eye Disease (TED) is an autoimmune disease that causes eye bulging (proptosis), double vision (diplopia) and may even cause blindness. US annual incidence, like I noted earlier, is 25,000 for active TED and 75,000 for chronic TED. Tepezza (teprotumumab) is the only approved drug for TED. Approved in January 2020, Tepezza is a monoclonal antibody that targets the insulin-like growth factor-1 receptor (IGF-1R), which is the same mechanism used by VRDN-001.

Among pipeline drugs, we have Immunovant's FcRn inhibitor, batoclimab, which has an ongoing phase 3 study. Argenx is running a Phase 3 trial of subcutaneous efgartigimod administered via prefilled syringe in adults with active, moderate-to-severe TED. In the same indication, Roche is also running two Phase 3 clinical trials of satralizumab. Thus, the TED space is heating up, and even if it gets approved, VRDN-001/003 need to differentiate themselves from Tepezza. TED is a multibillion dollar market. Tepezza, the only approved product, had nearly $2bn in sales in 2022. How VRDN-001/003 differentiates itself from Tepezza will be more important than only approval. Their having used placebo instead of tepezza in the control precludes a direct comparison. The primary endpoint, proptosis, saw similar data in both molecules. In some of the secondary endpoints, VRDN did better. For example, their 43% complete resolution of diplopia rate is much better than the 28% seen with tepezza. The chronic TED data is also a key differentiator.

Financials

VRDN has a market cap of $1.23bn and a cash balance of $717mn as of December. Research and development expenses were $238.3 million during the year ended December 31, 2024, while general and administrative expenses were $61.1 million. That gives them a cash runway of 6-7 quarters, or into the end of 2027, which conforms to the company's take on their cash runway.

Risks

The primary risk is that Tepezza is an established player from big pharma, and VRDN's placebo armed trial does not convince as much as a trial with tepezza in the control would have done. That was a big ask, and Viridian probably rightly did not venture there. However, that was still something investors would have wanted to see. This could be the reason VRDN is not trading as high as it should have been, given its outstanding data in a decent sized market.

Bottom line

I like VRDN for an approval, and we can worry about market penetration later. I think the data we have seen clearly marks VRDN-001 for a positive response from the FDA in about 18 months. That should be a major positive catalyst for the stock. I wouldn't expect too much from commercialization right now, given Tepezza's prowess in the space. That would come slowly, I guess. However, I expect some positive price movement on approval, and I am a buyer at these prices.

data.title: CFNAI Index Suggests Economy Is Slowing
data.description:
February Weakness And The Outlook For March

Last week, we discussed that continued bullish exuberance and high levels of complacency can quickly turn into volatility. Over the previous week, the market fell sharply following news of a potential viral outbreak in China and more concerns about tariffs from the Trump Administration. While those were the headlines that gained the market’s attention, the reality was that the market was overbought and on a sell signal, setting the market up for pullback. The correction was not unexpected, as discussed three weeks ago:

“The bullish bias is evident, as witnessed by the recent surge in retail money flows into leveraged ETFs and speculative options trading. However, as is always the case, whenever investors are crowded on “one side of the boat,” it is often a decent contrarian signal to be a bit more cautious. Furthermore, while there is currently no evidence of a catalyst for a correction, it is worth noting that we are entering into the seasonally weak part of February.”

In other words, while the media scrambled to align reasons with the sell-off, the correction was very much in line with seasonal tendencies. Crucially, that sell-off has pushed investor sentiment to levels typically equating to much deeper corrections. From a contrarian view, that extreme negative sentiment, now combined with oversold conditions, provides a good base for a rally in March.

“Sentiment is approaching two standard deviations below its average level. Such levels are more coincident with market bottoms than the beginning of a corrective cycle. I have labeled some events along the way. The lesson is that headlines drive sentiment, and when sentiment becomes too negative, as may be the case today, such allows for rallies to form.”

Here Comes March

Furthermore, March has a seasonal bias to the upside as we enter the end of the quarter and the last stretch of the seasonally strong period of the year. January, March, and April have the most substantial early year returns, with March and April having the highest win rates.

Does this mean the next major bull market rally is set to begin? No. But it does suggest that there are such high levels of negative sentiment that selling today will likely be a mistake. Loss avoidance is one of the psychological factors that leads investors to long-term underperformance. With market sentiment very negative and becoming decently oversold, be patient and wait for a tradeable rally to rebalance portfolio positions as needed. For traders, a decent tradable opportunity is likely in the near term.

However, while there is a potential for a decent tradeable rally over the next month or so, we still expect increased volatility this year. We want to reiterate using rallies to rebalance risk and manage allocations as needed.

The latest readings from the CFNAI index are one reason we remain cautious about our outlook.

The CFNAI Index Suggests The Economy Is Weakening

Recent economic reports have shown a more serious activity slowdown, which we have discussed many times in recent months. One indicator we watch closely is the Chicago Fed National Activity Index (CFNAI) which is arguably one of the most important and overlooked economic indicators. Each month, economists, the media, and investors pour over various mainstream economic indicators, from GDP to employment and inflation, to determine what markets will likely do next.

While economic numbers like GDP or the monthly non-farm payroll report typically garner the headlines, the most crucial statistic, in my opinion, is the CFNAI index. Investors and the press mostly ignore it, but the CFNAI is a composite index of 85 sub-components and provides a broad overview of overall economic activity in the U.S.

Since the beginning of this year, the markets have struggled as they digest news of tariffs, concerns over Artificial Intelligence, and sticky inflation. Even with that pickup in volatility, as discussed this past week, analysts are very optimistic about earnings growth throughout this year and into 2026. Those expectations are predicated upon continued robust economic growth. To wit:

“The chart below shows earnings’ long-term growth. Notably, analysts expect a record deviation above earnings’ long-term exponential growth trend of $195/share and above the 6% peak-to-peak historical growth rate.”

“The peak-to-peak growth trend is crucial because it is the historical growth rate of nominal GDP. Given that corporate revenue, from which earnings are derived, comes from economic activity, the correlation is logical. There are times when earnings can grow much faster than the economy, such as when the economy emerges from a recession. However, over time, earnings growth returns to the long-term relationship.”

However, if recent CFNAI index readings are any indication, investors may want to temper their expectations heading into this year. While most economic data series, like GDP, are backward-looking statistics, the CFNAI index is a forward-looking metric that indicates how the economy will likely look in the coming months. With a continued reading below zero, such does not suggest economic “robustness” in the months ahead.

Furthermore, the CFNAI index also supports the continued retracement of actual economic growth rates towards 2%, which has been the average since the turn of the century.

So, what is the CFNAI index telling us that the BEA economic reports are not?

CFNAI Index: Breaking Down The “Most Important Number”

Understanding the message the index is designed to deliver is critical. A negative reading does not equate to a recession. From the Chicago Fed website:

“The Chicago Fed National Activity Index (CFNAI Index) is a monthly index designed to gauge overall economic activity and related inflationary pressure. A zero value for the index indicates that the national economy is expanding at its historical trend rate of growth; negative values indicate below-average growth; and positive values indicate above-average growth.“

The overall index is broken down into four major sub-categories, which cover:

  • Production & Income
  • Employment, Unemployment & Hours
  • Personal Consumption & Housing
  • Sales, Orders & Inventories

I have constructed a four-panel chart better to understand the critical sub-components and their predictive capability. For illustrative purposes, I compare the CFNAI Index sub-components to the four most common economic reports: Industrial Production, Employment, Housing Starts, and Personal Consumption Expenditures. Notably, the CFNAI data is extremely volatile, so I have smoothed the data using a six-month average. Then, to align that data with the CFNAI index, I used an annual percentage change for the four mainstream economic components.

The correlation between the CFNAI index sub-components and the underlying major economic reports is high. This is why, even though this indicator gets very little attention, it represents the broader economy. The CFNAI index does not confirm the mainstream view of an “economic resurgence” that will drive earnings growth in 2026.

The EOCI Index Comparison

Furthermore, the CFNAI is also essential to our RIA Economic Output Composite Index (EOCI), which is an even broader composition of data points, including Federal Reserve regional activity indices, the Chicago PMI, ISM, the National Federation of Independent Business Surveys, and the Leading Economic Index. The EOCI also confirms that while economic data has improved following the economic shutdown, the “hopes” of a massive resurgence in economic activity are unlikely. Lastly, given that these indicators are only slightly into expansion territory, any impact from tariffs, deficit reductions, or increased unemployment will push these indicators back into contraction.

Notably, the EOCI and the Leading Economic Index remained in typically recessionary territory for an extended period, yet the National Bureau of Economic Research never called a recession. While readings have reverted to normal, the current state of broad economic data supports an economic growth rate of 2%-ish but not much more than that. Such a low economic growth rate does not support elevated levels of employment, increased wages, or consumption. Such is critical to the outlook for earnings.

“Again, this is because earnings are a function of economic activity. Therefore, for earnings to match current estimates, expectations for economic growth must improve sharply. However, such seems unlikely given the impact of tariffs and reductions in government spending and employment. Therefore, it is far more likely that we will eventually see a sharp decrease in forward estimates.”

The risk to investors is that if estimates are too elevated, any reduction in those estimates will weigh on asset prices as markets reprice for economic realities.

It’s In The Diffusion

The Chicago Fed also provides a breakdown of the change in the underlying 85 components in a “diffusion” index. As opposed to just the index itself, the “diffusion” of the components gives us a better understanding of the broader changes inside the index itself.

There are two points of consideration:

  1. When the diffusion index dips below zero, it coincides with weak economic growth and outright recessions.
  2. The S&P 500 has a history of corrections and outright bear markets, corresponding with negative readings in the diffusion index.

The second point should not be surprising, as the stock market reflects economic growth. Both the EOCI index above and the CFNAI below correlate to the annual rate of change in the S&P 500. However, what IS surprising is that since 2023, the market has surged higher despite a continued slate of economic weakness in actual activity. Such is because of the massive infusion of capital into the economy through fiscal and monetary measures. The break of the long-term correlation is remarkable and suggests that markets are likely to revert to underlying economic activity rather than the opposite.

That exact correlation also shows up in the annual growth rate of earnings. Given that earnings are a function of economic activity, as discussed above, the current deviation between earnings growth and the economy is likely unsustainable. Given that the Fed is maintaining a higher interest rate environment, inflation remains sticky, and the current Administration is taking disinflationary actions through immigration and deficit reduction policies, it is unlikely that the economy will rise to support current earnings growth rates.

Investors should also be concerned about the current level of consumer confidence readings.

Not So Confident

The chart below is our consumer confidence composite index. It combines the University of Michigan and the Conference Board’s sentiment readings into one index. Unsurprisingly, when consumers are confident, the stock market tends to rise. As Ben Bernanke noted, rising asset prices boost consumer confidence, supporting economic growth. While consumer confidence has risen from the 2022 lows, confidence readings are starting to show signs of stress as consumers run out of excess savings.

Crucially, consumer confidence feeds CEO confidence.

“Is it the consumer cranking out work hours, raising a family, and trying to make ends meet? Or the CEO of a company that has the best view of the economic landscape. Sales, prices, managing inventory, dealing with collections, paying bills, tells them what they need to know about the actual economy?”

In other words, when consumer confidence is high and demand for products or services is elevated, CEOs are willing to expend capital for expansion, employment, or increased wages. When consumer confidence reverses, CEO confidence will also reverse, leading to employment reductions, wage cuts, and reduced spending, which impacts economic growth. This is why, given that the market reflects confidence, investors should pay attention to what consumers and CEOs are saying and doing.

Despite the recent uptick in CEO confidence since October, which corresponded with strong equity market performance, confidence is hovering near peak expansionary levels.

The CFNAI also tells the same story: significant consumer confidence divergences eventually “catch down” to the underlying index, not vice versa.

Conclusion

If history is any guide, this chart suggests we will begin seeing weaker employment numbers and rising layoffs in the months ahead.

As noted, the data tells an important story for investors. Notably, the historical data of the CFNAI and its relationship to the stock market have included all Federal Reserve activity.

The CFNAI and EOCI incorporate the impact of monetary policy on the economy in both lagging and leading indicators. This is why investors should hedge risk to some degree in portfolios, as the data still suggests weaker than anticipated economic growth. The current trend of the various economic data points on a broad scale does not show indications of “economic resurgence” to support elevated earnings expectations. Instead, the data suggests an increased risk of disappointment in expectations.

Economically speaking, such weak levels of economic growth do not support more robust employment or higher wages. Instead, we should expect that 2025 could be a year in which corporate earnings and profits disappoint investors as economic weakness reasserts itself.

How We Are Trading It

Wall Street analysts’ overly optimistic assumptions are almost always likely to overshoot reality. As discussed, earnings tend to grow very close to economic activity, with overshoots and undershoots caused by macro-driven events like monetary and fiscal interventions or recessionary outcomes. This time is unlikely to be any different.

Given the current market exuberance, historical overvaluation concerns, and the reliance on buybacks to sustain prices, investors should consider hedging strategies to protect their portfolios.

  1. Don’t forget fixed income to lower market-related volatility.
  2. Don’t be afraid to increase cash levels when uncertain where to invest.
  3. Focus on fundamentally strong companies with strong dividend histories.
  4. Monitor corporate buybacks and economic indicators.

As we head into 2025, investors should expect market volatility to increase. Such is particularly true if buybacks decline due to economic weakness or policy changes. Likewise, tracking macroeconomic indicators such as employment data, inflation trends, and GDP growth can provide early warning signals of a market shift.

We continue to suggest taking small actions within portfolios. Such actions can help investors better navigate the risks associated with an unexpected rise in market volatility.

Or, you can shut your eyes and hope for the best.

SimpleVisor Top & Bottom Performers By Sector

S&P 500 Weekly Tear Sheet

Relative Performance Analysis

Last week, we noted the collision of options expiration, overbought markets, and news of a potential new viral outbreak in China sent markets lower.

“Notably, bonds are outperforming stocks for the year, with small, mid-cap, and discretionary stocks sporting a negative return. With the flight into safety over the last week, interest rate-sensitive markets are overbought, and growth markets are oversold.We could see a bit of counter-rotation early next week, but that will depend on how the absorbs the risk of another viral outbreak.”

While the outbreak news was quickly dismissed, stocks struggled this week, with bonds, real estate, healthcare, financials, and staples outperforming. Those sectors are now very overbought, and profits should be harvested. Conversely, technology is oversold enough to likely see the rally that started on Friday continue this week.

Technical Composite

The technical overbought/sold gauge comprises several price indicators (R.S.I., Williams %R, etc.), measured using “weekly” closing price data. Readings above “80” are considered overbought, and below “20” are oversold. The market peaks when those readings are 80 or above, suggesting prudent profit-taking and risk management. The best buying opportunities exist when those readings are 20 or below.

The current reading is 52.47 out of a possible 100.

Portfolio Positioning “Fear / Greed” Gauge

The “Fear/Greed” gauge is how individual and professional investors are “positioning” themselves in the market based on their equity exposure. From a contrarian position, the higher the allocation to equities, the more likely the market is closer to a correction than not. The gauge uses weekly closing data.

NOTE: The Fear/Greed Index measures risk from 0 to 100. It is a rarity that it reaches levels above 90.The current reading is 62.50 out of a possible 100.

Relative Sector Analysis

Most Oversold Sector Analysis

Sector Model Analysis & Risk Ranges

How To Read This Table

  • The table compares the relative performance of each sector and market to the S&P 500 index.
  • “MA XVER” (Moving Average Crossover) is determined by the short-term weekly moving average crossing positively or negatively with the long-term weekly moving average.
  • The risk range is a function of the month-end closing price and the “beta” of the sector or market. (Ranges reset on the 1st of each month)
  • The table shows the price deviation above and below the weekly moving averages.

The risk/range report has reset for the beginning of the month. However, I want to restate the following from last week:

“One of the early warning indicators the risk-range report provides is the moving average crossover (last column) of intermediate and long-term measures. A few months ago, only bonds and international traded on bearish crossovers. Since then, we have sharply increased the number of bearish crossovers in various sectors and markets. Such suggests an ongoing deterioration in price action in the markets, which has yet to spill over into the S&P 500 completely. While this does not mean that investors should immediately become bearish, it does suggest that risks are building for a more substantial market correction at some point if these sub-markets do not start to improve soon.”

Weekly SimpleVisor Stock Screens

We provide three stock screens each week from SimpleVisor.

This week, we are searching for the Top 20:

  • Relative Strength Stocks
  • Momentum Stocks
  • Fundamental & Technical Strength W/ Dividends

RSI Screen

Momentum Screen

Technically Strong With Buy Rating

SimpleVisor Portfolio Changes

We post all of our portfolio changes as they occur at SimpleVisor:

Feb 26th

“This morning, we are doing a tax loss harvest and swapping in the portfolio. We are selling all of AMD (AMD), which has been underperforming, and swapping it into Arista Networks (ANET). This swap provides two purposes to the portfolio. First, it diversifies the portfolio into hardware on the technology side, to which we had no exposure. Secondly, it reduces our exposure to semiconductors ahead of the Nvidia (NVDA) earnings report tonight.

If NVDA reports well, that report should benefit ANET, which is very oversold, and AMD. However, a poor report would likely impair AMD more than ANET. While we still like AMD, the swap reduces the portfolio risk overall.”

Equity Model

  • Sell 100% of AMD
  • Initiate a 1% of the portfolio position in Arista Networks (ANET)

Original Post

data.title: Customers Bancorp Isn't Ready For A Downgrade Yet
data.description:
One of the beautiful things about value investing is that while it is preferred that the companies you buy into are continuously growing, they don't necessarily have to be in order to be appealing. A good example of this can be seen by looking at Customers Bancorp (NYSE:CUBI), a financial firm with a market capitalization as of this writing of $1.67 billion. Even though the balance sheet of the company is expanding at a nice pace, revenue and profits have come under pressure as of late. Even though this is disappointing, I have remained bullish on the firm.

Back in October of last year, I decided to reaffirm my ‘buy’ rating on the stock. I acknowledged at the time that the business was facing some issues. But because of how shares were priced, I argued that additional upside was warranted. Since then, shares have risen by 14.2% at a time when the S&P 500 is up only 2.1%. And since I originally rated the company a ‘buy’ in August 2023, shares are up 53.7%. That's comfortably above the 35.8% upside achieved by the S&P 500. I will admit that I am getting closer to downgrading the stock. However, because of how shares remain from a valuation perspective, I believe that keeping it as a soft ‘buy’ makes sense at the moment.

Still worth keeping in mind

Operationally speaking, Customers Bancorp is far from the smallest bank that I have analyzed. It's also far from the largest. The firm has 73 different locations as of this writing, with those locations focused on major states such as New York, Pennsylvania, New Jersey, and elsewhere. Like most banks, the company offers services to the communities in which it operates. On the consumer side of things, it provides access to checking and savings accounts, personal loans, and more. It also offers up residential mortgages, multifamily and investment commercial real estate loans, SBA loans, commercial and industrial loans, and more. It even engages in certain specialized banking services such as venture banking and fund financing.

When I last wrote about the company, we had access to data covering through the second quarter of the 2024 fiscal year. But results now extend through the final quarter of the same year. During that quarter, net interest income for the bank came in at $146.6 million. That represents a decline compared to the $159 million reported one year earlier. Some of this decline was driven by a rise in the provision for credit losses from $13.5 million to $21.2 million. Overall, the company suffered from a drop in its net interest margin from 3.31% to 3.11%. I have noticed that declining net interest margins have been an issue in the banking sector in this era of high interest rates. This is because when interest rates are high, depositors look for yield elsewhere. So banks have to ratchet up how much they are paying depositors in order to keep or grow deposits. For the year as a whole, a drop in net interest margin from 3.29% to 3.15% helped to cause a drop in net interest income from $612.8 million to $581 million.

This wasn't the only weakness for the company. Non-interest income in the final quarter of 2023 was $18.7 million. During the final quarter of 2024, this metric came in negative to the tune of $0.2 million. This is in spite of the fact that the firm saw growth in some key areas. For instance, commercial lease income popped from $9 million to $10.6 million. Loan fees grew from $5.9 million to $8.6 million. However, the pain for the company was the result of a $26.3 million loss on the sale of investment securities during the final quarter of the year. This, sadly, was instrumental in bringing down the firm's non-interest income for 2024 as a whole to $60.4 million compared to the $70.6 million it stood at the previous year. Even though the business enjoyed an $11.4 million unrealized gain on equity method investments during this time, this was more than offset by the loss on the sale of investment securities and by a $14.4 million increase in the loss on the sale of loans and leases.

Because of these weaknesses, net profits for the firm contracted from $58.2 million in the final quarter of 2023 to $23.3 million in the final quarter of 2024. It also doesn't help that total non-interest expense for the company jumped from $93.8 million to $110.4 million because of higher expenses across key areas. As an example, salaries and employee benefits costs jumped from $34 million to $47.1 million. For the year as a whole, net profits totaled $166.4 million, compared to the $235.4 million reported the previous year.

Even though we saw a drop in revenue and profits during this time, the business has seen a return to growth when it comes to its balance sheet. After seeing deposits dropped from $17.92 billion in 2023 to $17.68 billion in the second quarter of 2024, there was a rise to $18.85 billion in the final quarter of last year. $777.1 million of this deposit increase occurred from the third quarter of the year to the fourth quarter.

Whenever I see a bank returned to growth and I see this occur at a time when net interest margin is falling, I immediately look at whether the change was driven by brokered deposits or not. These are deposits that come from other financial institutions, and they do cost more than other traditional deposits. However, management said that the more than $1 billion of gross deposit inflows that the company achieved in the final quarter of the year were utilized, in part, to reduce higher cost and brokered deposits. In fact, for the final quarter of the year, non-interest-bearing deposits jumped by $937.5 million. This is most certainly a net positive as I see it.

Other aspects of the balance sheet deserve attention as well. For the final quarter of 2024, loans for the business totaled $14.31 billion. That's up from the $12.73 billion the company reported one year earlier. During the same window of time, the value of securities did contract from $3.51 billion to $3.01 billion. Cash also fell from $3.85 billion to $3.79 billion. However, this all came at a time when debt contracted as well, from $1.51 billion to $1.41 billion. On the whole, I would say that all of these balance sheet changes together are a net positive, especially the loan and deposit data that matters most.

When it comes to valuing the company, one approach that we can use is to look at its price to earnings multiple. However, because of the various one-time changes that negatively impacted earnings, this might give us a distorted picture. The good news is that management did provide what they refer to as core adjusted earnings. For 2024, this figure came in at $189.3 million. Using this figure, we can see in the chart above how the stock is priced compared to five similar firms. And in this regard, shares are cheaper than any of these competitors. Even if we use the official earnings, the price to earnings multiple of 10 that the company is trading for is cheaper than these other businesses and happens to be at the very high end of what I consider to be the attractive range for banks.

In the next chart above, I also valued the company using both the price to book and price to tangible book multiples. In both of these instances, you can see that our candidate is, once again, the cheapest of the group. Of course, we should also be paying attention to asset quality. In this regard, Customers Bancorp is fine, but it's not great. In the first chart below, you can see its return on assets compared to these five other companies. Three of the five have a reading higher than it does. The exact same thing holds true in the subsequent chart with return on equity. So in this regard, Customers Bancorp is a decent company, but not a great one.

Takeaway

Even though times have been a bit difficult for Customers Bancorp, the company has returned to growth where it matters most. Deposits and loans are growing while debt is decreasing. Shares are attractively priced. Admittedly, asset quality is not the best. But it's certainly not bad either. Given this combination of factors, I do think that Customers Bancorp still deserves a soft ‘buy’ rating at this time.

data.title: Recession Fears May Be Overblown - This Week's Data Could Prove It
data.description:
The sharp drop in Treasury rates may have been an overreaction to recession fears. This week’s data will put that narrative to the test. If the numbers align with analysts' forecasts, rates are likely to rebound and ease concerns about an economic slowdown.

ISM Data

The ISM manufacturing report is expected to come on Monday, and analysts estimate that the index will fall to 50.5 from 50.9. But even a tick lower in this index would be the second month of expansion in the manufacturing sector.

But perhaps, more important, will be the prices paid column because last month saw prices paid rise to 54.90 from 52.50. However, there is a lot of evidence from regional Fed manufacturing surveys showing prices rose significantly in February. The Philadelphia, Empire State, Kansas City, and Dallas Fed surveys showed their respective prices paid indexes all rose above the highs seen in the spring of 2024, which is suggestive of the ISM prices paid index rising above 60 in February, which would be a massive jump from January.

There was also this commentary from the ISM Chicago Business Survey on Friday, which noted:

Prices Paid leapt 16.9 points. This is the largest monthly increase since July 1957, bringing the index to its highest level since August 2022. No respondents reported lower prices, whilst over half reported higher prices.

The Job Report

Of course, the February job report will have the most significant impact, and that comes on Friday. As of February 28, analysts are forecasting 153,000 jobs to have been created in February, up from 143,000 in January. Meanwhile, the unemployment rate is expected to remain unchanged at 4%. Wage growth is expected to increase by 0.3% m/m, down from a blistering 0.5% in January; year-over-year wage growth is forecast to remain at 4.1%.

When looking at the expectations for this coming week, it seems hard to imagine that we are amid a significant slowdown. Of course, most of the rate decline started on February 21 when the S&P Global Services PMI came in at 49.7, well below expectations for 53. That same day, the University of Michigan showed weaker-than-expected consumer confidence, while long-term inflation expectations rose to their highest levels since 1995.

Not All Assets Agree

One can easily imagine how quickly the market's mind could change this coming week should the data presented come in as expected or perhaps better than expected. This could lead to a big reversal in interest rates as the market reassesses the current recession fears.

Despite the drop in interest rates, the dollar has remained strong. Usually, when we think about a weakening economy, we think about rates falling and the dollar weakening as well. While the dollar is off its recent highs, it hasn't fallen nearly as profoundly as the 10-year rate has declined.

If it should continue, the recent reversal in the dollar is likely to suggest that the recent decline in interest rates isn't likely to persist.

Since September, we have also seen 1-, 2-, and 5-year inflation swaps trade higher. Inflation swaps are a good guide for the inflation outlook and the growth outlook because inflation and growth generally go hand in hand. But more recently, inflation swaps have moved to their highest levels in more than two years. Importantly, they tend to trend with rates, suggesting the recent decline in rates may be overdone.

It is possible that we are entering a stagflationary period, but that would also argue for an increase in the unemployment rate, and at least based on the current estimates for February, that is not expected. Additionally, we have yet to see a meaningful increase in unemployment claims or continuing claims, and it would suggest that unemployment is unlikely to change much in February.

Based on the current Department of Government Efficiency plans, it is estimated that 200,000 people could lose their jobs. Assuming no changes to the size of the labor force, the unemployment rate would only rise to around 4.1% from its current 4%. This would return the total number of unemployed workers to around November 2024 levels.

While the economy can shift quickly, current forecasts show little indication of a significant slowdown. If recession fears prove overblown again, traders may be forced to unwind bad bets, driving rates sharply higher.

data.title: KONE: Better Options With Less China In The Mix
data.description:
KONE Oyj (OTCPK:KNYJF) continues to benefit from margin improvement as mentioned in our previous coverage thanks to their razor and blades business model with the pressures mainly at the top of the funnel and mainly in China. A pretty significant restructuring program incurred costs this year accounting for over 10% of the Q4 operating profits, and this has been focused on right-sizing the China business as it deals with a tough environment brought on by the property market deleveraging which should create durable incremental profit improvement even in that weak market. The top of the funnel businesses are not vigorous, but the margin and profit resilience, and the resilience of profit growth, continue for KONE as it does for the rest of the industry. However, we prefer Schindler (OTC:SHLRF) where forward multiples are cheaper and there is less China exposure. Deleveraging economies don't usually see a quick rebound, it usually takes at least two years for a recovery to start occurring or more depending on the full extent of the excesses, and there has been too much optimism at every announcement of stimulus in China.

Latest Earnings

Ex-China, order growth was solid at 10% across all business lines, with modernization activities and services leading the way, which are also the businesses that drive margin. Even in China, modernization activity didn't look too bad, whereas new building solutions, the top of the funnel revenues that carry very low margins but create the base for service and modernization revenues later which do bring in profits, were down 2.9% driven by the pressure from the Chinese real estate situation. As a reminder, the wind down of major Chinese developers and the related economic woes with having an economy that is heavily tied up in real estate and construction has had a severe and deleterious effect on the economy, exacerbated by the fact that property was one of the few savings sinks on offer for Chinese households. Orders overall in China driven by new building solutions were down 20%, and sales consequently were down by 15%. There is no escaping the fact that this is having a pressure on operating profits as well, even if the new building solutions business is not a very profitable activity.

Services overall grew by more than 10%, which is the high margin and recurring business. Service revenues happen on an entirely fixed schedule determined by building managers, and it carries the margins. Performance of this segment almost entirely drives the positive mix effects that leads to a significant growth in profits, at least on an adjusted basis.

There was over 50 million EUR in restructuring costs logged in the Q4 related mostly, but not entirely (probably around 30 million EUR), to rightsizing the business in China and to restore the business's marginality amid expectations for the pressure on Chinese activities to continue. This should produce some enduring cost benefits incrementally. Comps will also get a bit easier in China, but the pricing and volume issue continues there, and the traumatic effect that the real estate wind down has had on the economy could endure easily for another year. Meanwhile, other markets like North America are picking up a little ahead of Europe on the new building signs, with increasing optimism around construction for residential projects in particular, though we aren't betting on too strong of a recovery because rates are probably not coming down much more. APMEA, as a more emerging destination market, is firing more solidly across all the segments.

Bottom Line

China is not a market that is favorable, even against the weak comps. Europe may be benefiting from the end of the Ukraine war soon, with investors already hunting for return-to-normal deals around sanctions. The US also seems to be picking up slowly, though we are not expecting rate cuts to expedite development plans in the US. In all markets, it is clear that service and even modernization revenues remain a reliable source of recurring and profitable revenue. However, we don't think KONE is especially good value. While cheaper than Otis (OTIS), which may also be justified in its premium valuation given lesser exposure to China, KONE is more expensive than Schindler (15.6x EV/EBITDA compared to 14.9x on a forward basis), which similarly has a lack of exposure to China like Otis (carrying a more European tilt). While the US may be more vigorous than Europe, both are better exposures than China, which other than the deleveraging also have to worry about a mounting trade war which could seriously impact the US export sink on which the economy quite meaningfully depends, particularly if the US responds with further retaliatory tariffs, where a 50% drop in exports to the US could occur as forecast by Bloomberg. This would draw out China's economic pains. So we prefer Schindler here clearly over KONE.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: RSP: S&P 500 Monthly Dashboard For March
data.description:
This article reports sector metrics in the S&P 500 index. It is also a top-down analysis of ETFs based on the S&P 500, like SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) and Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight ETF (NYSEARCA:RSP).

RSP strategy and historical performance

Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight ETF was launched on 04/24/2003 and holds all S&P 500 stocks in equal weight, rebalanced quarterly. RSP has slightly outperformed the capital-weighted fund SPY in total return since 5/1/2003. Nonetheless, the gap in annualized return is insignificant (7 bps). RSP is more volatile and shows a lower Sharpe ratio (risk-adjusted performance) than the benchmark.

From 5/1/2003

RSP would have beaten SPY since January 2000, using synthetic prices based on the underlying index before inception. However, it has lagged it over the last 10 years (RSP: 163.6% - SPY: 235.6%).

As for fundamentals, RSP has value characteristics. It is cheaper than SPY based on valuation ratios and has a lower earnings growth rate.

Source: Fidelity

Shortcut

The next two paragraphs in italic describe the dashboard methodology. They are necessary for new readers to understand the metrics. If you are used to this series or if you are short of time, you can skip them and go to the charts.

Base Metrics

I calculate the median value of five fundamental ratios in every sector: Earnings Yield ("EY"), Sales Yield ("SY"), Free Cash Flow Yield ("FY"), Return on Equity ("ROE"), Gross Margin ("GM"). All are calculated on trailing 12 months. For all these ratios, higher is better and negative is bad. EY, SY and FY are medians of the inverse of Price/Earnings, Price/Sales and Price/Free Cash Flow. They are better for statistical studies than price-to-something ratios, which are unusable when the "something" is close to zero or negative (for example, companies with negative earnings). I also calculate two momentum metrics for each group: the median monthly return (RetM) and the median annual return (RetY).

I prefer medians rather than averages because a median splits a set in a good half and a bad half. Capital-weighted averages are skewed by extreme values and the largest companies. As a consequence, these metrics are designed for stock-picking rather than index investing.

Value and Quality Scores

Historical baselines are calculated as the averages on a look-back period of 11 years for all metrics. They are noted respectively EYh, SYh, FYh, ROEh, GMh. For example, the value of EYh for technology in the table below is the 11-year average of the median Earnings Yield of S&P 500 tech companies.

The Value Score "VS" is the average difference in % between the three valuation ratios (EY, SY, FY) and their baselines (EYh, SYh, FYh). The same way, the Quality Score "QS" is the average difference between the two quality ratios (ROE, GM) and their baselines (ROEh, GMh).

VS may be interpreted as the percentage of undervaluation or overvaluation relative to the baseline (positive is good, negative is bad). This interpretation must be taken with caution: the baseline is an arbitrary reference, not a supposed fair value. The formula assumes that the three valuation metrics are of equal importance, except in energy and utilities where the Free Cash Flow Yield is ignored to avoid some inconsistencies. VS and QS are capped between -100 and +100 when the calculation goes beyond these values.

Interpretation

A hypothetical S&P 500 “median” company is overvalued by 14.7% relative to averages since 2014, with a quality score slightly above the historical baseline. Median yields can be translated into their inverse ratios:

  • Price/Earnings: 26.25
  • Price/Sales: 3.07
  • Price/Free Cash Flow: 45.05

Energy has been the sector with the best value and quality scores since February 2022. Consumer staples and communication services are undervalued by 6% to 7% relative to 11-year averages. Healthcare and real estate are almost on their historical baseline. Consumer discretionary and financials are overvalued by less than 10%. Materials and utilities are between 15% and 20% of overvaluation. Technology and industrials are the most overvalued sectors, at about 26% and 28%, respectively.

RSP has a 12-month total return of 11.6%, while SPY is at 17.3%. It is a confirmation that mega-cap companies have significantly skewed the capital-weighted index to the upside over the last 12 months.

Cheap stocks among their peers

We use the table above to calculate value and quality scores. It may also be used in a stock-picking process to check how companies stand among their peers. For example, the EY column tells that a large consumer staples company with an Earnings Yield above 0.0463 (or price/earnings below 21.60) is in the better half of the sector regarding this metric. A Dashboard List is sent every month to Quantitative Risk & Value subscribers, with the most profitable companies standing in the better half among their peers regarding the three valuation metrics at the same time.
